Neural networks can be constructed using the torch.nn package.  
A neural network depends on autograd to define models and differentiate them.

An nn.Module contains layers, and a method forward(input) that returns the output.

![][image1]

A typical training procedure for a neural network is as follows:

- Define the neural network that has some learnable parameters (or weights)  
- Iterate over a dataset of inputs  
- Process input through the network  
- Compute the loss (how far is the output from being correct)  
- Propagate gradients back into the network’s parameters  
- Update the weights of the network  
  - Simple update rule: weight \= weight \- learning\_rate \* gradient

Say we define this network:

| import torch import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F class Net(nn.Module):     def \_\_init\_\_(self):         super(Net, self).\_\_init\_\_()         \# 1 input image channel, 6 output channels, 5x5 square convolution         \# kernel         self.conv1 \= nn.Conv2d(1, 6, 5)         self.conv2 \= nn.Conv2d(6, 16, 5)         \# an affine operation: y \= Wx \+ b         self.fc1 \= nn.Linear(16 \* 5 \* 5, 120)  \# 5\*5 from image dimension         self.fc2 \= nn.Linear(120, 84)         self.fc3 \= nn.Linear(84, 10)     def forward(self, input):        \# Convolution layer C1: 1 input image channel, 6 output channels,         \# 5x5 square convolution, it uses RELU activation function, and         \# outputs a Tensor with size (N, 6, 28, 28), where N is the size of the batch         c1 \= F.relu(self.conv1(input))         \# Subsampling layer S2: 2x2 grid, purely functional,         \# this layer does not have any parameter, and outputs a (N, 6, 14, 14\) Tensor         s2 \= F.max\_pool2d(c1, (2, 2))         \# Convolution layer C3: 6 input channels, 16 output channels,         \# 5x5 square convolution, it uses RELU activation function,         \# and outputs a (N, 16, 10, 10\) Tensor         c3 \= F.relu(self.conv2(s2))         \# Subsampling layer S4: 2x2 grid, purely functional,         \# this layer does not have any parameter, and outputs a (N, 16, 5, 5\) Tensor         s4 \= F.max\_pool2d(c3, 2)         \# Flatten operation: purely functional, outputs a (N, 400\) Tensor         s4 \= torch.flatten(s4, 1)         \# Fully connected layer F5: (N, 400\) Tensor input,         \# and outputs a (N, 120\) Tensor, it uses RELU activation function         f5 \= F.relu(self.fc1(s4))         \# Fully connected layer F6: (N, 120\) Tensor input,         \# and outputs a (N, 84\) Tensor, it uses RELU activation function         f6 \= F.relu(self.fc2(f5))         \# Fully connected layer OUTPUT: (N, 84\) Tensor input, and         \# outputs a (N, 10\) Tensor         output \= self.fc3(f6)         return output net \= Net() print(net) |
| :---- |

The output is as follows:

| Net(   (conv1): Conv2d(1, 6, kernel\_size=(5, 5), stride=(1, 1))   (conv2): Conv2d(6, 16, kernel\_size=(5, 5), stride=(1, 1))   (fc1): Linear(in\_features=400, out\_features=120, bias=True)   (fc2): Linear(in\_features=120, out\_features=84, bias=True)   (fc3): Linear(in\_features=84, out\_features=10, bias=True) ) |
| :---- |

You would have to define the forward function, and the backward function (where gradients are computed) is automatically defined for you using autograd.  
Any Tensor operation can be used for the forward function.  
The learnable parameters of a model are returned by net.parameters().

| Input | params \= list(net.parameters()) print(len(params)) print(params\[0\].size()) \# conv1’s weight |
| :---- | :---- |
| Output | 10 torch.Size(\[6, 1, 5, 5\]) |

We can try with a random 32 by 32 input.  
Note that the MNIST dataset must be converted to the right dimensions first.

| Input | input \= torch.randn(1, 1, 32, 32\) out \= net(input) print(out) |
| :---- | :---- |
| Output | tensor(\[\[-0.1649,  0.0338,  0.1873,  0.1751, \-0.0093, \-0.0097,  0.1403, \-0.0990, 0.0222,  0.0980\]\], grad\_fn=\<AddmmBackward0\>) |

Zero the gradient buffers of all parameters and backprops with random gradients.

| net.zero\_grad() out.backward(torch.randn(1, 10)) |
| :---- |

Recap:

- torch.Tensor → a multi-dimensional array with support for autograd operations.  
  - Also holds the gradient with respect to the tensor.  
- nn.Module → neural network module.  
  - Convenient way of encapsulating parameters.  
  - Includes helpers for moving them to GPU, exporting, loading, etc.  
- nn.Parameter → a type of tensor that is automatically registered as a parameter when assigned as an attribute to a Module.  
- autograd.Function → implements forward and backward definitions of an autograd operation.  
  - Every tensor operation creates at least a single function node that connects to functions that created a tensor and encodes its history.

Loss function → takes the (output, target) pair of inputs, and computes a value that estimates how far away the output is from the target.

There are several different loss functions under the nn package.  
A simple loss is: nn.MSELoss, which computes the mean-squared error between the output and the target.

For example,

| Input | output \= net(input) target \= torch.randn(10) \# a dummy target, for example target \= target.view(1, \-1) \# reshape to output’s shape criterion \= nn.MSELoss() loss \= criterion(output, target) print(loss) |
| :---- | :---- |
| Output | tensor(0.7390, grad\_fn=\<MseLossBackward0\>) |

Now, if we follow the loss in the backward direction, using its .grad\_fn attribute, you will see a graph of computations that looks like this:

| input \-\> conv2d \-\> relu \-\> maxpool2d \-\> conv2d \-\> relu \-\> maxpool2d       \-\> flatten \-\> linear \-\> relu \-\> linear \-\> relu \-\> linear       \-\> MSELoss       \-\> loss |
| :---- |

So, when we call loss.backward(), the whole graph is differentiated with respect to the neural net parameters, and all tensors in the graph that have requires\_grad=True will have their .grad tensor accumulated with the gradient.

For illustration, we can walk a few steps backward.

| Input | print(loss.grad\_fn) \# MSELoss print(loss.grad\_fn.next\_functions\[0\]\[0\]) \# linear print(loss.grad\_fn.next\_functions\[0\]\[0\].next\_functions\[0\]\[0\])\# ReLU |
| :---- | :---- |
| Output | \<MseLossBackward0 object at 0x7f7a39f6d5a0\> \<AddmmBackward0 object at 0x7f7a39f6f700\> \<AccumulateGrad object at 0x7f7a39c66c50\> |

Backpropagation → to backpropagate the error, all we have to do is loss.backward().

- Need to clear existing gradients, or else gradients will be accumulated.

| Input | net.zero\_grad() \# zeroes the gradient buffers of all params print(‘conv1.bias.grad before backward:’) print(net.conv1.bias.grad) loss.backward() print(‘conv1.bias.grad after backward:’) print(net.conv1.bias.grad) |
| :---- | :---- |
| Output | Conv1.bias.grad before backward: None Conv1.bias.grad after backward: tensor(\[-0.0067, \-0.0058, \-0.0300, 0.0014, \-0.0101, \-0.0121\]) |

We need to update weights to keep the pipeline flexible  
The simplest update rule used in practice is the Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD):

| weight \= weight \- learning\_rate \* gradient |
| :---- |

We can implement this using simply Python code:

| learning\_rate \= 0.01 for f in net.parameters():     f.data.sub\_(f.grad.data \* learning\_rate)) \# subtract |
| :---- |

However, as you use neural networks, you want to use various different update rules.  
They can include SGD, Nesterov-SGD, Adam, RMSProp, etc.  
To enable this, we can use the package torch.optim that implements all these methods.

| import torch.optim as optim \# create optimizer optimizer \= optim.SGD(net.parameters(), lr \= 0.01) \# in your training loop: optimizer.zero\_grad() \# zero the gradient buffers output \= net(input) loss \= criterion(output, target) loss.backward() optimizer.step() \# does the update |
| :---- |

[image1]: <data:image/png;base64,>
